description: Causative 'ka' adds a causer: the subject brings about what another participant does or becomes. The means may be forceful or enabling; 'ka' itself states the causal relation.
articulatory_notes: The rear of the tongue closes at the soft palate for 'k' and releases into open-central 'a'. Full stress falls on the sole syllable.
usage_notes: With an intransitive predicate, the original subject becomes the caused participant; with a transitive predicate, that participant still acts on the original object. The only two-particle voice sequence is 'se ka'. Aspect before 'ka' describes the causing, while 'ka ma' denies that the causing occurred.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Putting 'ka' in the clause keeps the causer audible even when another participant performs the visible action. The sentence can then discuss that influence instead of crediting only the performer.
